growth_records.agit: 190 rpm (growth-rate/competition assays); 200 rpm during electroporation recovery; 190 rpm preculture
growth_records.medium: PC+ medium (modified PC medium; iron-depleted artificial/natural seawater base) + 500 nM FeCl3
growth_records.cite: Hogle et al. Direct Heme Uptake by Phytoplankton-Associated Roseobacter Bacteria. mSystems (2017). DOI:10.1128/mSystems.00124-16. [PMC5225302]
growth_records.snippet: The specific growth rate of TM1040 in an iron-depleted medium with a 500 nM concentration of added inorganic FeCl3 was 0.31 ± 0.02 h−1
growth_records.notes: Value directly reported in Results (mean ± SD, n=3 replicates, from OD600 growth curves, Fig. 3/Table S1). Temperature recorded as 'room temperature' in Methods (approximated here as 22°C); exact pH of seawater-based PC+ medium not stated.

growth_records.agit: 190 rpm (growth-rate/competition assays); 200 rpm during electroporation recovery; 190 rpm preculture
growth_records.medium: PC+ medium (modified PC medium; iron-depleted seawater base), no added iron source
growth_records.cite: Hogle et al. Direct Heme Uptake by Phytoplankton-Associated Roseobacter Bacteria. mSystems (2017). DOI:10.1128/mSystems.00124-16. [PMC5225302]
growth_records.snippet: it was significantly lower (0.13 ± 0.004 h−1) in the absence of a supplemented iron source
growth_records.notes: Value directly reported in Results (mean ± SD, n=3 replicates). Same base PC+ medium as the FeCl3 record but with no added iron source (iron-limited).

growth_records.agit: 190 rpm (growth-rate/competition assays); 200 rpm during electroporation recovery; 190 rpm preculture
growth_records.medium: PC+ medium (modified PC medium; iron-depleted seawater base) + 500 nM heme (hemin chloride)
growth_records.cite: Hogle et al. Direct Heme Uptake by Phytoplankton-Associated Roseobacter Bacteria. mSystems (2017). DOI:10.1128/mSystems.00124-16. [PMC5225302]
growth_records.snippet: TM1040 grew at a level comparable to that seen under FeCl3 conditions when we provided 500 nM heme as the iron source (0.28 ± 0.01 h−1)
growth_records.notes: Value directly reported in Results (mean ± SD, n=3 replicates).

growth_records.agit: 190 rpm (growth-rate/competition assays); 200 rpm during electroporation recovery; 190 rpm preculture
growth_records.medium: PC+ medium (modified PC medium; iron-depleted seawater base) + 17 µg/mL hemoglobin
growth_records.cite: Hogle et al. Direct Heme Uptake by Phytoplankton-Associated Roseobacter Bacteria. mSystems (2017). DOI:10.1128/mSystems.00124-16. [PMC5225302]
growth_records.snippet: TM1040 was able to grow as well with Hb (0.28 ± 0.01 h−1) as with FeCl3 but not cyt c (0.14 ± 0.01 h−1)
growth_records.notes: Value directly reported in Results (mean ± SD, n=3 replicates).

growth_records.agit: 190 rpm (growth-rate/competition assays); 200 rpm during electroporation recovery; 190 rpm preculture
growth_records.medium: PC+ medium (modified PC medium; iron-depleted seawater base) + 500 nM cytochrome c
growth_records.cite: Hogle et al. Direct Heme Uptake by Phytoplankton-Associated Roseobacter Bacteria. mSystems (2017). DOI:10.1128/mSystems.00124-16. [PMC5225302]
growth_records.snippet: TM1040 was able to grow as well with Hb (0.28 ± 0.01 h−1) as with FeCl3 but not cyt c (0.14 ± 0.01 h−1)
growth_records.notes: Value directly reported in Results (mean ± SD, n=3 replicates); covalently-bound heme in cytochrome c is poorly bioavailable, giving a growth rate close to the no-iron condition.

growth_records.strain: MC1-AF10 (population MC1; coral-associated denitrifying specialist; isolated from Oulastrea crispata)
growth_records.agit: 150 rpm (isotope assay); coral skeleton homogenate vortexed 800 rpm during sampling
growth_records.medium: Custom nutrient-depleting marine medium (Supplementary Data 16) + 0.1 mmol/l 15N-labelled sodium nitrate as sole electron acceptor

up.method: 0.1 mmol/l 15N-NaNO3 added as sole electron acceptor; net nitrate consumption rate not quantified in paper

sec.method: 15N stable-isotope labelling; headspace N2 by purge-and-trap GasBench-Precon IRMS (ThermoScientific Delta V Plus), 4 h incubation

growth_records.cite: Xiang, Nan et al. Decoding coral resistance to eutrophication through the association of hyper-efficient denitrifiers as key microbial allies. Nature Communications (2026). DOI:10.1038/s41467-026-72571-w. [PMC13187470]
growth_records.method: not reported (resting-cell activity assay, not a growth-rate measurement)
growth_records.snippet: The top four highest 30N2 productions were found in four isolates belonging to four different denitrifying specialist MCs, i.e., MC1-AF10 (472.5 pmoles ml-1 h-1), MC6-O11 (212.30 pmoles ml-1 h-1), MC13-L12 (165.88 pmoles ml-1 h-1), and MC18
growth_records.notes: Experimental denitrification product (30N2, two 15N atoms) secretion rate 472.5 pmol/ml/h reported verbatim in Results and Fig.3c. Assay: 10^6 CFU/ml resting cells, 0.1 mmol/l 15N-NaNO3 sole e-acceptor, nanoxic (0.5 umol/l DO, argon), 28 C, 150 rpm, 4 h. Rate is per ml culture (not gDW-normalized); biomass ~10^6 CFU/ml. 45N2O/29N2 (single 15N) were 10-20x lower. Not an MFA/GEM paper; a marine microbial-ecology isotope-activity study.
